返回本批次

S3 文件

S3 Files

www.allthingsdistributed.com 作者 werner 分数 216 评论 64 2026/04/08 03:44
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2026 年 4 月 7 日 • 6004 字图片来源：Ossewa 几乎每个人在其职业生涯的某个阶段都经历过将大量数据从一个地方移动到另一个地方的令人深感沮丧的过程，如果您没有，那么您可能只是还没有使用足够大的数据集。对于 Andy Warfield 来说，影响深远的经历之一是在 UBC 与基因组学研究人员一起工作，他们产生了大量的测序数据，但在将数据获取到需要的地方的机制上花费了荒谬的大量时间。永远来回复制数据，管理多个不一致的副本。这个问题令各个行业的构建者感到沮丧，从实验室的科学家到训练机器学习模型的工程师，而这正是我们应该为客户解决的问题类型。在这篇文章中，Andy 介绍了他的团队提出的解决方案：S3 文件。来之不易的教训，一些真正有趣的时刻，以及至少一次失败的命名新数据类型的尝试。这是一本引人入胜的读物，我想您会喜欢的。–W第 1 部分：S3 的变化首先，一些植物学事实证明，向日葵比人类更加杂乱。大约十年前，就在加入亚马逊之前，我完成了我的第二家创业公司并回到了 UBC 任教。我想探索一些我没有太多研究经验的东西，并决定学习基因组学，特别是计算机系统和生物学家如何进行基因组学研究的交叉点。我最后和 UBC 植物学教授 Loren Rieseberg 一起度过了一段时光，他研究向日葵 DNA，通过分析基因组来了解植物如何发展出使它们在干旱或盐碱土壤等挑战性环境中茁壮成长的性状。植物学家关于滥交的笑话（这篇博客的开头就是这个笑话）是与 Loren 的实验室合作如此有趣的原因之一。他们的解释是，人类 DNA 大约有 30 亿个碱基对，任何两个人在基因组水平上都是 99.9% 相同的——我们所有的 DNA 都非常相似。但向日葵作为花朵，并不是一夫一妻制的，它拥有更大的基因组（大约 36 亿个碱基对）和更多的变异（个体之间的遗传变异是其 10 倍）。当时我的一位博士毕业生 JS Legare 决定和我一起踏上这次冒险之旅，并继续在 Loren 的实验室做博士后，探索如何将这些工作负载转移到云端。基因组分析是一些研究人员称之为“突发并行”计算的一个例子。 DNA 分析可以通过大量并行计算来完成，并且当您这样做时，它通常会运行相对较短的时间。这意味着在实验室中使用本地硬件可能不太合适，因为您通常没有足够的计算能力来在需要时运行快速分析，并且当您不进行积极工作时，您拥有的计算能力会处于闲置状态。我们的想法是探索使用 S3 和无服务器计算并行运行数万或数十万个任务，以便研究人员可以非常快速地运行复杂的分析，然后在完成后将规模缩小到零。生物学家在 Linux 中使用名为 GATK4 的分析框架，这是一个与 Apache Spark 集成的基因组分析工具包。他们的所有数据都位于共享 NFS 文件管理器上。在连接到云的过程中，JS 构建了一个他称为“bunnies”（另一个混杂笑话）的系统，将分析打包在容器中并在 S3 上运行，这通过并行化在速度、可重复性和性能方面取得了真正的胜利。但一个突出的教训是存储边界处的摩擦。S3 在并行性、成本和耐用性方面非常出色，但基因组学研究人员使用的每个工具都期望本地 Linux 文件系统。研究人员总是来回复制数据，管理多个有时不一致的副本。这种数据摩擦（一侧是 S3，另一侧是文件系统，中间是手动复制管道）是我多年来反复看到的情况。在媒体和娱乐、机器学习预训练、芯片设计和科学计算中。不同的工具被编写为以不同的方式访问数据，当位于我们数据前面的 API 成为摩擦源，使工作变得更加困难时，这很糟糕。代理放大了数据摩擦我们都知道，而且我认为甚至可能有点震惊，代理工具正在改变当今的软件开发方式。特工们非常擅长编写代码，而且他们的进步速度足够快，以至于我们都花了相当多的时间来思考这一切意味着什么（甚至是维尔纳）。但看起来确实如此的一件事是，代理开发已经深刻地改变了构建应用程序的成本。成本以美元、时间、尤其是与编写可行代码相关的技能而言。我发现最后一部分是最令人兴奋的最近，因为自从我们拥有软件以来，成功的应用程序总是涉及将两种经常脱节的技能组合在一起：一方面是正在编写的应用程序领域的技能，如基因组学、金融或设计，另一方面是实际编写代码的技能。在很多方面，代理都说明了编写软件的准入门槛一直以来都高得令人望而却步，并且突然允许更多的人来编写应用程序——这些人在所编写的应用程序领域拥有深厚的技能，而不是在编写应用程序的机制方面。当我们发现自己正处于一个比以往更快、更具实验性、更多样化的应用程序编写阶段时，从想法到运行代码的周期时间正在急剧压缩。随着构建应用程序的成本大幅下降，并且我们构建的每个应用程序都可以作为下一个应用程序的参考，确实感觉代码/数据划分变得比以往任何时候都更有意义。我们正在进入一个应用程序来来去去的时代，并且一如既往，数据比所有应用程序都更长寿。有效的存储系统的作用始终不仅是安全地存储数据，而且还有助于将其抽象并与单个应用程序解耦。随着应用程序开发步伐的加快，存储的这一特性变得比以往任何时候都更加重要，因为数据越容易附加和使用，我们就越能发挥、构建和探索从中受益的新方法。S3 作为数据的管家在过去的几年里，S3 团队一直非常关注最后一点。我们一直在密切关注 S3 中数据访问方式不够简单的情况，就像 Loren 实验室的生物学家必须构建脚本来复制数据，以便将数据放在正确的位置以与他们的工具一起使用一样，我们开始更广泛地关注客户发现使用存储会分散他们处理数据的注意力的地方。我们在这里学到的第一课是结构化数据。 S3 存储 EB 级的 Parquet 数据，平均每秒对该格式的请求超过 2500 万个。其中很多要么是普通的镶木地板，要么是像 Hive 桌子一样结构化。很明显，人们希望利用这些数据做更多事情。开放表格式（尤其是 Apache Iceberg）作为功能更丰富的表抽象而出现，允许插入和突变、模式更改和表快照。虽然 Iceberg 显然有助于提升 S3 上表格数据的抽象级别，但它仍然具有一系列尖锐的边缘，因为它必须严格在对象 API 上显示表格。随着 Iceberg 开始越来越受欢迎，大规模采用它的客户告诉我们，管理安全策略很困难，他们不想管理表维护和压缩，并且希望更轻松地处理表格数据。此外，Iceberg 和开放表格式 (OTF) 上的大量工作通常是专门为 Spark 驱动的。虽然 Spark 作为分析引擎非常重要，但人们将数据存储在 S3 中，因为他们希望能够使用他们想要的任何工具来处理它，甚至（尤其是！）尚不存在的工具。因此，在 2024 年，我们在 re:Invent 上推出了 S3 表，作为托管的一流表原语，可以充当结构化数据的构建块。 S3 Tables 将数据存储在 Iceberg 中，但添加了护栏以保护数据完整性和持久性。它使压缩自动化，增加了对跨区域表复制的支持，并继续完善和扩展这样的想法：表应该是与对象并存的一流数据原语，作为构建应用程序的一种方式。如今，我们在 S3 表中存储了超过 200 万个表，并且看到在它们之上构建了各种出色的应用程序。大约在同一时间，我们开始与 S3 客户就相似性搜索和向量索引进行大量对话。过去几年人工智能的进步确实为各种存储数据的矢量索引创造了机会和需求。先进的嵌入模型提供了这个机会，该模型在提供语义搜索的能力方面引入了阶跃功能变化。突然之间，拥有大量档案媒体收藏（例如历史体育镜头）的客户可以建立矢量索引，并实时搜索得分跳水达阵的特定球员，并立即获得剪辑集合，组装成热门卷轴，可用于现场直播。语义相关搜索的相同属性对于 RAG 以及在未经训练的数据上应用模型同样有价值。随着客户开始在其数据上构建和操作向量索引，他们开始强调略有不同的数据摩擦来源。强大的载体数据库已经存在，并且载体已经被很快就成为 Postgres 等现有数据库的一项功能。但这些系统将索引存储在内存或 SSD 中，作为具有实时索引的计算集群运行。这是连续低延迟搜索设施的正确模型，但如果您从存储角度访问数据，那么它的帮助就较小。客户发现，尤其是代码或 PDF 等基于文本的数据，向量本身通常比索引数据的字节数更多，存储在介质上的成本要高很多倍。因此，就像团队使用 S3 表处理结构化数据一样，在上次 re:Invent 中，我们推出了 S3 Vectors 作为向量索引的新 S3 原生数据类型。 S3 Vectors 在存储向量方面采用了非常 S3 的风格，因为它的设计基于与 S3 对象非常相似的性能、成本和耐用性配置文件。也许最重要的是，S3 Vectors 被设计为完全弹性的，这意味着您可以快速创建仅包含数百条记录的索引，并随着时间的推移扩展到数十亿条记录。 S3 Vector 的最大优势实际上在于拥有始终可用的 API 端点，可以支持相似性搜索索引，非常简单。就像对象和表一样，它是您可以在应用程序开发中使用的另一种数据原语。现在...S3 文件今天，我们推出了 S3 文件，这是一项新的 S3 功能，它将 Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) 集成到 S3 中，并允许将任何现有的 S3 数据作为网络附加文件系统直接访问。关于文件的故事实际上比表或向量的工作更长，甚至更有趣，因为文件是一种复杂而棘手的数据类型。与对象存储干净地集成。实际上，在推出 S3 Tables 之前，我们就开始研究文件的想法，这是 EFS 和 S3 团队之间的共同努力，但让我们暂时先讨论一下这个问题。正如我在分析向日葵 DNA 的基因组学示例中所描述的那样，有大量现有软件通过文件系统 API、数据科学工具、构建系统、日志处理器、配置管理和训练管道来处理数据。如果您看过代理编码工具处理数据，您会发现它们很快就可以使用丰富的 Unix 工具来直接处理本地文件系统中的数据。处理 S3 中的数据意味着加深他们必须主动列出 S3 中的文件、将它们传输到本地磁盘，然后对这些本地副本进行操作的推理。它显然比代理用例更广泛，对于当今工作中使用本地文件系统的每个客户应用程序来说都是如此。 S3 上的本机支持文件使所有数据立即更易于访问，并最终更有价值。您无需从 S3 中复制数据即可在其上使用 pandas，或向其指定训练作业，或使用设计工具与其进行交互。使用 S3 文件，您会得到一件非常简单的事情。现在，您可以在 EC2 VM、容器或 Lambda 函数内挂载任何 S3 存储桶或前缀，并通过文件系统访问该数据。如果您进行更改，您的更改将传播回 S3。因此，您可以将对象视为文件，将文件视为对象。这就是故事变得有趣的地方，因为正如我们在尝试让客户变得简单时经常了解到的那样，使事情变得简单往往是您可以着手做的更复杂的事情之一。第 2 部分：S3 文件的设计 构建者讨厌这样一个事实：他们必须尽早决定他们的数据是存在于文件系统还是对象存储中，并且从那时起就必须承担由此产生的后果。有了这个决定，他们基本上会选择如何与数据交互，不仅是现在，而且是在未来很长一段时间内，如果他们出错了，他们要么必须进行迁移，要么构建一个自动化层来复制数据。早期的想法基本上是，我们将 EFS 和 S3 放在一个大锅里，稍微煮一会儿，然后我们就可以两全其美。我们甚至将该项目的早期版本称为“EFS3”（我很高兴我们没有保留这个名称！）。但事情很快就变得棘手了。每次我们坐下来进行设计时，我们都会发现困难的技术挑战和艰难的决定。在每一个决策中，数据的文件或对象表示都必须在设计中放弃一些东西，这会使其变得不太好。团队中的一位工程师将其描述为“一场令人难以接受的妥协之战”。我们并不是第一批发现将文件和对象融合到单个存储系统中是多么困难的存储人员，但我们也敏锐地意识到，没有解决问题的方案会让构建者感到多么沮丧。我们决心找到解决问题的途径，所以当您面临真正困难的技术设计问题时，我们做了唯一明智的事情：我们把一群最高级的工程师锁在一个房间里，并说，除非他们有一个他们都喜欢的计划，否则我们不会让他们出去。随之而来的是激烈而有争议的讨论。随之而来的是。随之而来的是。最终我们放弃了。我们只是无法找到一个不会让某些人（在大多数情况下实际上是每个人）对设计不满意的解决方案。此时顺便说一句：我可能对将人们锁在房间里的评论采取了一些戏剧性的自由。亚马逊的会议室没有锁。但要明确这一点：我经常发现，当我们让聪明、热情、拥有不同技术观点的人们在白板前真正深入研究几天时，我们在真正困难的设计问题上取得了最快和最具建设性的进展。这并不是一个惊天动地的观察，但令人惊讶的是，在试图通过视频会议一小时讨论重大难题时，很容易忘记。这些讨论中的工程师深入了解文件和对象工作负载以及它们之间差异的微妙之处，因此这些讨论很深入，有时很激烈，而且绝对令人着迷。尽管如此，我们仍然无法得到我们喜欢的设计。这真的很令人沮丧。那是 2024 年圣诞节前后。临近假期，团队改变了路线。他们浏览了他们拥有的设计文档和讨论笔记，并开始列举所有具体的设计妥协以及如果我们想要将文件和对象接口呈现为一个统一的系统，我们需要适应的行为。我们都研究过它，并一致认为这不是两全其美的，而是最低的公分母，我们都可以想到双方的示例工作负载，这些工作负载会以令人惊讶的、通常微妙的、总是令人沮丧的方式崩溃。我认为这个对我来说真正突出的例子是关于对象和文件作为数据原语实际上如何不同的顶级语义和经验。这是一个极其简单的特征：文件是操作系统的构造。它们存在于存储中，并且在断电时仍然存在，但是当使用它们时，它们作为表示数据的方式非常丰富，以至于它们非常频繁地用作跨线程、进程和应用程序通信的方式。文件的应用程序 API 旨在支持这样的想法：我可以就地更新数据库中的记录，或将数据附加到日志中，并且您可以同时访问该文件并几乎立即查看我对文件的任意子区域的更改。有一组丰富的操作系统功能，例如 mmap()，它可以将文件作为共享持久数据加倍，这些数据可以以非常细的粒度发生变化，就好像它是一组内存中的数据结构。现在，如果我们转向对象世界，在其他人访问对象时写入对象中间的想法或多或少是亵渎的。对象的不变性是 API 和应用程序中的一个假设。工具将下载并验证内容哈希值，它们将使用对象版本控制来保留旧副本。最值得注意的是，他们经常构建复杂的工作流程，这些工作流程完全基于与整个对象创建相关的通知。当我开始研究 S3 时，最后一件事让我感到惊讶，它实际上非常酷。 S3 跨区域复制 (CRR) 等系统根据创建或覆盖对象时发生的通知来复制数据，并且这些通知被认为具有至少一次语义，以确保我们永远不会错过对象的复制。客户使用类似的管道来触发日志处理、图像转码和各种其他东西——这是基于对象的应用程序设计的一种非常流行的模式。事实上，通知是 S3 子系统的一个示例，它让我对我所使用的存储系统的规模感到惊叹：S3 每天仅向处理新对象的无服务器事件侦听器发送超过 3000 亿个事件通知！我们逐渐意识到，文件和对象之间实际上存在相当深刻的界限。文件交互是敏捷的，通常变化很大，并且语义丰富。另一方面，对象具有相对集中且狭窄的语义集。我们意识到，分隔它们的边界才是我们真正需要注意的，而不是试图隐藏它，边界本身就是我们需要构建的功能。阶段和提交当我们从假期回来时，我们开始再次将人们锁定（好吧，不完全锁定）房间里，但这一次的观点是文件和对象之间的边界实际上不必是不可见的。这一次，团队在讨论结束后看起来更加高兴。第一个决定是我们打算将 S3 上的一流文件访问视为处理数据的表示层。我们将允许客户在存储桶或前缀上定义 S3 挂载，并且在幕后，该挂载将附加 EFS 命名空间以镜像来自 S3 的元数据。我们将使两层之间的数据传输和一致性成为我们设计的绝对核心部分。我们开始将其描述为“阶段和提交”，这是我们从 git 等版本控制系统借用的一个术语——更改将能够在 EFS 中累积，然后集中推送到 S3——并且数据如何以及何时传输边界的细节将作为系统的一部分发布，对客户来说是清楚的，并且随着时间的推移，我们实际上可以作为编程原语继续发展和改进。 （我将在最后详细讨论这一点，因为团队很高兴能在这个表面上做更多的事情）。当团队开始研究 S3 Files 时，我根本没有想到会明确文件和对象表示之间的界限，这也是我真正喜欢这个设计的地方。现在还为时过早，我们还有很大的发展空间，但我认为团队都认为这为我们奠定了一条道路，我们很高兴与构建者的需求合作改进和发展，而不是陷入那些令人不快的妥协之中。尚未脱离困境在这个阶段做出决定并提交事情是提供一些边界和关注点分离的设计决策之一。它给了我们一个清晰的结构，但并没有让难题消失。团队仍然必须在文件和对象语义、性能和一致性之间进行真正的权衡。让我通过几个例子来展示这两个抽象实际上有多么微妙，以及团队如何处理这些决策。一致性和原子性S3 读者经常假设完整的对象更新、通知，并且在许多情况下访问历史版本。文件系统具有细粒度的突变，但它们也具有重要的一致性和原子性技巧。许多应用程序依赖于原子文件重命名的能力，作为使大型更改一次性可见的一种方式。他们对目录移动做了同样的事情。 S3 条件对第一件事有一定帮助，但并不完全匹配，并且第二件事没有 S3 模拟。如上所述，分离层允许这些模式在并行系统中共存，并具有相同数据的单一视图。您可以随意更改和重命名文件，稍后，它将作为一个整体写入 S3。AuthorizationAuthorization 同样棘手。 S3 和文件系统以非常不同的方式考虑授权。 S3 支持范围为键前缀的 IAM 策略 - 您可以说“拒绝对 /private/ 下的任何内容进行 GetObject”。事实上，您可以根据网络或请求本身的属性等因素进一步限制这些权限。 IAM 策略非常丰富，而且评估成本也比文件权限高得多。文件系统花费了数年时间从数据路径中获取诸如权限检查之类的内容，通常会预先进行评估，然后使用句柄进行持久的未来访问。文件作为包装授权策略的实体也有点奇怪，因为文件的权限位于其 inode 中。硬链接允许您对同一文件拥有多个 inode，并且您还需要考虑目录权限，以确定您是否可以首先访问文件。除非你掌握了它，在这种情况下，即使它被重命名、移动，甚至经常被删除，也没关系。这里要讨论的复杂性、呃、丰富性要多得多，尤其是围绕用户和组身份等主题，但通过转向明确的边界，团队就不必在每个单个对象上共同表示两种类型的权限。相反，可以在挂载本身（网络文件系统用户熟悉的领域）上指定权限，并在文件系统内强制执行，并在两个世界中应用特定的映射。这种设计还有另一个优点。它保留了 S3 上的 IAM 策略作为后盾。如果需要更改数据边界，您始终可以禁用 S3 层的访问，同时将授权委派给每个挂载中的文件层。它为将来我们可能想要探索相同数据上的多个不同挂载的情况敞开了大门。命名空间语义的可怕的不一致如果您熟悉文件和对象系统，那么考虑文件和对象命名行为完全不同的情况并不困难。当你开始坐下来真正深入研究时，事情会变得近乎可笑的荒凉。文件系统具有一流的路径分隔符——通常是正斜杠（“/”）字符。 S3 也有这些，但它们实际上只是一个建议。事实上，S3LIST 命令允许您指定任何要解析为路径分隔符的内容，并且有少数客户已经构建了出色的多维命名结构，这些结构在同一路径中嵌入多个不同的分隔符，并根据他们想要组织结果的方式将不同的分隔符传递给 LIST。这是另一个简单而烦人的问题：因为 S3 没有目录，所以您可以拥有以相同斜杠结尾的对象。也就是说，你可以拥有一个看起来像目录但实际上是文件的东西。在大约 20 分钟的时间里，团队认为这是一个很酷的功能，并称它们为“文件目录”。谢天谢地，我们没有保留那个。这些差异有数十个，我们仔细考虑了限制为一个共同的结构，或者只是将自己固定在一侧或另一侧。在所有这些路径上，我们意识到我们将打破有关应用程序内部命名的假设。我们决定倾向于边界并允许双方坚持现有的命名约定和语义。当创建了无法跨越边界移动的对象或文件时，我们决定（哇，这是一个非常激烈的讨论）我们不会移动它们。相反，我们会发出一个事件，以便客户进行监控并在必要时采取行动。这显然是一个将复杂性交给开发人员的例子，但我认为这也是一个很好的例子，说明这是正确的做法，因为我们选择不在他们已经期望运行的域中失败，我们正在构建一个边界，允许绝大多数路径名在这两种情况下都有效，并且我们正在构建一个机制来检测和纠正出现的问题。 性能体验团队花了很多时间讨论的最后一个主要差异领域是性能，特别是性能和命名空间交互的请求延迟。文件和对象命名空间针对截然不同的事物进行了优化。在文件系统中，存在许多与数据相关的元数据访问。访问文件也意味着访问（在某些情况下更新）目录记录。还有许多操作最终会沿着路径遍历所有目录记录。因此，快速文件系统名称空间（即使是大型分布式文件系统名称空间）往往会将目录的所有元数据共同定位在单个主机上，以便这些交互尽可能快。对象命名空间是完全扁平的，并且倾向于针对非常高度并行的点查询和更新进行优化。在 S3 中的许多情况下，单个“目录”中包含数十亿个对象，并且被数十万个客户端并行访问。当我们审视我刚才描述的一系列挑战时，我们花了很多时间讨论采用。 S3 已经有二十年历史了，我们想要一种现有 S3 客户可以立即使用他们自己的数据的解决方案，而不是意味着迁移到全新的东西。有大量现有存储桶为应用程序提供服务，这些应用程序依赖于 S3 的对象语义，其工作方式与文档完全一致。我们不愿意引入可能破坏这些应用程序的微妙的新行为。事实证明，很少有应用程序在同一时刻对相同数据同时使用文件和对象接口。更常见的模式是多相。数据处理管道在一个阶段使用文件系统工具来生成输出，供下一阶段基于对象的应用程序使用。或者客户想要对通过文件系统主动修改的数据快照运行分析查询。我们意识到没有必要融合文件和对象语义来解决数据孤岛问题。他们需要的是在一个地方提供相同的数据，并为每种访问模式提供正确的视图。提供完全 NFS 接近打开一致性的文件视图。提供完整 S3 原子 PUT 强一致性的对象视图。以及一个让它们保持连接的同步层。所以我们交付了它。所有这些争论——团队列出的“令人不快的妥协”清单，关于文件目录的热情和偶尔荒凉的讨论——结果证明正是我们需要做的工作。我认为团队都认为经过设计后效果更好。 S3 文件允许您将任何 S3 存储桶或前缀作为文件系统安装在 EC2 实例、容器或 Lambda 函数上。它在幕后由 EFS 提供支持，可提供您的工具所期望的文件体验。 NFS 语义、目录操作、权限。从应用程序的角度来看，它是一个已安装的目录。从 S3 的角度来看，数据是存储桶中的对象。它的工作方式值得快速浏览一下。当您首次访问目录时，S3 Files 会从 S3 导入元数据并填充同步视图。对于 128 KB 以下的文件，它还会提取数据本身。对于较大的文件当您实际读取数据时，只有元数据会过来，并且数据是从 S3 获取的。这种惰性水合作用很重要，因为这意味着您可以安装一个装有数百万个物体的桶，然后立即开始工作。这个“立即开始工作”部分是一个简单体验的好例子，但实际上在幕后却相当复杂——能够以文件形式挂载并立即使用 S3 中的对象是对该功能的明显而自然的期望，并且必须等待几分钟或几小时才能填充元数据的文件视图，这将是相当令人沮丧的。但在幕后，S3 Files 需要扫描 S3 元数据并为其填充文件优化的命名空间，团队能够非常快速地实现这一点，并且作为保留简单且非常敏捷的客户体验的后台操作。当您创建或修改文件时，更改将被聚合并大约每 60 秒作为单个 PUT 提交回 S3。同步是双向运行的，因此当其他应用程序修改存储桶中的对象时，S3 Files 会自动发现这些修改并自动将它们反映在文件系统视图中。如果同时从两个位置修改文件而发生冲突，则 S3 是事实来源，文件系统版本会移动到具有标识该事件的 CloudWatch 指标的丢失+发现目录。 30 天内未访问的文件数据将从文件系统视图中逐出，但不会从 S3 中删除，因此存储成本与您的活动工作集成正比。在团队构建系统时，发生了许多较小且非常有趣的工作。我认为非常酷的改进之一就是我们所说的“读取旁路”。对于高吞吐量顺序读取，读取旁路会自动重新路由读取数据路径，不使用传统的 NFS 访问，而是直接向 S3 本身执行并行 GET 请求，这种方法可实现每个客户端 3 GB/s（还有进一步改进的空间），并可跨多个客户端扩展到每秒太比特。对于那些感兴趣的人，我们的技术文档中有更多详细信息（这是一本非常有趣的读物）。我真正欣赏该设计的一件事是它对自身优势的诚实程度。文件域和对象域之间的明确边界并不是我们要掩盖的限制。这是让双方都保持不妥协的事情。也就是说，我们知道在某些地方仍有工作要做。重命名的成本很高，因为 S3 没有本机重命名操作，因此重命名目录意味着复制和删除该前缀下的每个对象。正是出于这个原因，当安装覆盖超过 5000 万个对象时，我们会向您发出警告。启动时不存在显式提交控制； 60 秒的窗口适用于大多数工作负载，但我们知道这不足以满足所有人的需求。有些对象键根本无法表示为有效的 POSIX 文件名，因此它们不会出现在文件系统视图中。我们已经进行了大约九个月的客户测试，这些是我们学到的东西，并继续与早期客户一起发展和迭代。我们宁愿清楚地了解它们，而不是假装它们不存在。 文件和向日葵当我们与 UBC 的 Loren 实验室合作时，JS 花费了大量时间构建缓存和命名层 - 不是做生物学，而是编写基础设施以在数据所在位置和工具预期位置之间传输数据。这种摩擦对我来说确实很突出，现在回顾起来，我认为我们在那个实验室中不断吸取的教训，然后在 S3 团队研究表、向量和现在的文件时一遍又一遍地吸取的经验教训是，处理数据的不同方式并不是一个可以解决的问题。它们是需要服务的现实。 Loren 实验室中的向日葵在变化中茁壮成长，事实证明数据访问模式也是如此。我发现 S3 文件最令人兴奋的是我在开始时真正没想到的事情：文件和对象之间的明确边界被证明是设计中最好的部分。我们花了几个月的时间试图让它消失，当我们最终接受它作为系统的一流元素时，一切都变得更好了。阶段和提交为我们提供了一个可以继续发展的表面 - 对数据传输边界的时间和方式进行更多控制，与管道和工作流程进行更丰富的集成 - 它使我们能够在不损害任何一方的情况下做到这一点。20 年前，S3 最初是一个对象存储。在过去的几年里，随着表格、向量和现在的文件的出现，它变得更广泛了。数据可以持久保存并且可以以任何对当前工作有意义的方式进行处理的地方。我们的目标是让存储系统不再妨碍您的工作，而不是成为您必须解决的问题。我们还远远没有完成，但我对我们前进的方向感到非常兴奋。正如沃纳所说，“现在，开始建造吧！”

Original Summary

  1. April 07, 2026 • 6004 wordsPhoto credit: OssewaAlmost everyone at some point in their career has dealt with the deeply frustrating process of moving large amounts of data from one place to another, and if you haven’t, you probably just haven’t worked with large enough datasets yet. For Andy Warfield, one of those formative experiences was at UBC, working alongside genomics researchers who were producing extraordinary volumes of sequencing data but spending an absurd amount of their time on the mechanics of getting that data where it needed to be. Forever copying data back and forth, managing multiple inconsistent copies. It is a problem that has frustrated builders across every industry, from scientists in the lab to engineers training machine learning models, and it is exactly the type of problem that we should be solving for our customers.In this post, Andy writes about the solution that his team came up with: S3 Files. The hard-won lessons, a few genuinely funny moments, and at least one ill-fated attempt to name a new data type. It is a fascinating read that I think you’ll enjoy.–WPart 1: The Changing Face of S3 First, some botanyIt turns out that sunflowers are a lot more promiscuous than humans. About a decade ago, just before joining Amazon, I had wrapped up my second startup and was back teaching at UBC. I wanted to explore something that I didn’t have a lot of research experience with and decided to learn about genomics, and in particular the intersection of computer systems and how biologists perform genomics research. I wound up spending time with Loren Rieseberg, a botany professor at UBC who studies sunflower DNA—analyzing genomes to understand how plants develop traits that let them thrive in challenging environments like drought or salty soils.The botanists’ joke about promiscuity (the one that started this blog) was one reason why Loren’s lab was so fun to work with. Their explanation was that human DNA has about 3 billion base pairs, and any two humans are 99.9% identical at a genomic level—all of our DNA is remarkably similar. But sunflowers, being flowers, and not at all monogamous, have both larger genomes (about 3.6 billion base pairs) and way more variation (10 times more genetic variation between individuals).One of my PhD grads at the time, JS Legare, decided to join me on this adventure and went on to do a postdoc in Loren’s lab, exploring how we might move these workloads to the cloud. Genomic analysis is an example of something that some researchers have called “burst parallel” computing. Analyzing DNA can be done with massive amounts of parallel computation, and when you do that it often runs for relatively short periods of time. This means that using local hardware in a lab can be a poor fit, because you often don’t have enough compute to run fast analysis when you need to, and the compute you do have sits idle when you aren’t doing active work. Our idea was to explore using S3 and serverless compute to run tens or hundreds of thousands of tasks in parallel so that researchers could run complex analysis very very quickly, and then scale down to zero when they were done.The biologists worked in Linux with an analytics framework called GATK4—a genomic analysis toolkit with integration for Apache Spark. All of their data lived on a shared NFS filer. In bridging to the cloud, JS built a system he called “bunnies” (another promiscuity joke) to package analyses in containers and run them on S3, which was a real win for velocity, repeatability, and performance through parallelization. But a standout lesson was the friction at the storage boundary.S3 was great for parallelism, cost, and durability, but every tool the genomics researchers used expected a local Linux filesystem. Researchers were forever copying data back and forth, managing multiple, sometimes inconsistent copies. This data friction—S3 on one side, a filesystem on the other, and a manual copy pipeline in between—is something I’ve seen over and over in the years since. In media and entertainment, in pretraining for machine learning, in silicon design, and in scientific computing. Different tools are written to access data in different ways and it sucks when the API that sits in front of our data becomes a source of friction that makes it harder to work with.Agents amplify data frictionWe are all aware, and I think still maybe even a little stunned, at the way that agentic tooling is changing software development today. Agents are pretty darned good at writing code, and they are getting better at it fast enough that we’re all spending a fair bit of time thinking about what it all even means (even Werner). One thing that does really seem true though is that agentic development has profoundly changed the cost of building applications. Cost in terms of dollars, in terms of time, and especially in terms of the skill associated with writing workable code. And it’s this last part that I’ve been finding the most exciting lately, because for about as long as we’ve had software, successful applications have always involved combining two often disjointed skillsets: On one hand skill in the domain of the application being written, like genomics, or finance, or design, and on the other hand skill in actually writing code. In a lot of ways, agents are illustrating just how prohibitively high the barrier to entry for writing software has always been, and are suddenly allowing apps to be written by a much larger set of people–people with deep skills in the domains of the applications being written, rather than in the mechanics of writing them.As we find ourselves in this spot where applications are being written faster, more experimentally, more diversely than ever, the cycle time from idea to running code is compressing dramatically. As the cost of building applications collapses, and as each application we build can serve as a reference for the next one, it really feels like the code/data division is becoming more meaningful than it has ever been before. We are entering a time where applications will come and go, and as always, data outlives all of them. The role of effective storage systems has always been not just to safely store data, but also to help abstract and decouple it from individual applications. As the pace of application development accelerates, this property of storage has become more important than ever, because the easier data is to attach to and work with, the more that we can play, build, and explore new ways to benefit from it.S3 as a steward for your dataOver the past few years, the S3 team has been really focused on this last point. We’ve been looking closely at situations where the way that data is accessed in S3 just isn’t simple enough–precisely like the example of biologists in Loren’s lab having to build scripts to copy data around so that it’s in the right place to use with their tooling–and we started looking more broadly at places where customers were finding that working with storage was distracting them from working with data. The first lesson that we had here was with structured data. S3 stores exabytes of parquet data and averages over 25 million requests per second to that format alone. A lot of this was either as plain parquet or structured as Hive tables. And it was clear that people wanted to do more with this data. Open table formats, notably Apache Iceberg, were emerging as functionally richer table abstractions allowing insertions and mutations, schema changes, and snapshots of tables. While Iceberg was clearly helping lift the level of abstraction for tabular data on S3, it also still carried a set of sharp edges because it was having to surface tables strictly over the object API.As Iceberg started to grow in popularity, customers who adopted it at scale told us that managing security policy was difficult, that they didn’t want to have to manage table maintenance and compaction, and that they wanted working with tabular data to be easier. Moreover, a lot of work on Iceberg and Open Table Formats (OTFs) generally was being driven specifically for Spark. While Spark is very important as an analytics engine, people store data in S3 because they want to be able to work with it using any tool they want, even (and especially!) the tools that don’t exist yet. So in 2024, at re:Invent, we launched S3 Tables as a managed, first-class table primitive that can serve as a building block for structured data. S3 Tables stores data in Iceberg, but adds guardrails to protect data integrity and durability. It makes compaction automatic, adds support for cross-region table replication, and continues to refine and extend the idea that a table should be a first-class data primitive that sits alongside objects as a way to build applications. Today we have over 2 million tables stored in S3 Tables and are seeing all sorts of remarkable applications built on top of them.At around the same time, we were beginning to have a lot of conversations about similarity search and vector indices with S3 customers. AI advances over the past few years have really created both an opportunity and a need for vector indexes over all sorts of stored data. The opportunity is provided by advanced embedding models, which have introduced a step-function change in the ability to provide semantic search. Suddenly, customers with large archival media collections, like historical sports footage, could build a vector index and do a live search for a specific player scoring diving touchdowns and instantly get a collection of clips, assembled as a hit reel, that can be used in live broadcast. That same property of semantically relevant search is equally valuable for RAG and for applying models over data they weren’t trained on.As customers started to build and operate vector indexes over their data, they began to highlight a slightly different source of data friction. Powerful vector databases already existed, and vectors had been quickly working their way in as a feature on existing databases like Postgres. But these systems stored indexes in memory or on SSD, running as compute clusters with live indices. That’s the right model for a continuous low-latency search facility, but it’s less helpful if you’re coming to your data from a storage perspective. Customers were finding that, especially over text-based data like code or PDFs, that the vectors themselves were often more bytes than the data being indexed, stored on media many times more expensive.So just like with the team’s work on structured data with S3 Tables, at the last re:Invent we launched S3 Vectors as a new S3-native data type for vector indices. S3 Vectors takes a very S3 spin on storing vectors in that its design anchors on a performance, cost and durability profile that is very similar to S3 objects. Probably most importantly though, S3 Vectors is designed to be fully elastic, meaning that you can quickly create an index with only a few hundred records in it, and scale over time to billions of records. S3 Vector’s biggest strength is really with the sheer simplicity of having an always-available API endpoint that can support similarity search indices. Just like objects and tables, it’s another data primitive that you can just reach for as part of application development.And now… S3 FilesToday, we are launching S3 Files, a new S3 feature that integrates the Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) into S3 and allows any existing S3 data to be accessed directly as a network attached file system.The story about files is actually longer, and even more interesting than the work on either Tables or Vectors, because files turn out to be a complex and tricky data type to cleanly integrate with object storage. We actually started working on the files idea before we launched S3 Tables, as a joint effort between the EFS and S3 teams, but let’s put a pin in that for a second.As I described with the genomics example of analyzing sunflower DNA, there is an enormous body of existing software that works with data through filesystem APIs, data science tools, build systems, log processors, configuration management, and training pipelines. If you have watched agentic coding tools work with data, they are very quick to reach for the rich range of Unix tools to work directly with data in the local file system. Working with data in S3 means deepening the reasoning that they have to do to actively go list files in S3, transfer them to the local disk, and then operate on those local copies. And it’s obviously broader than just the agentic use case, it’s true for every customer application that works with local file systems in their jobs today. Natively supporting files on S3 makes all of that data immediately more accessible—and ultimately more valuable. You don’t have to copy data out of S3 to use pandas on it, or to point a training job at it, or to interact with it using a design tool.With S3 Files, you get a really simple thing. You can now mount any S3 bucket or prefix inside your EC2 VM, container, or Lambda function and access that data through your file system. If you make changes, your changes will be propagated back to S3. As a result, you can work with your objects as files, and your files as objects.And this is where the story gets interesting, because as we often learn when we try to make things simple for customers, making something simple is often one of the more complicated things that you can set out to do.Part 2: The Design of S3 Files Builders hate the fact that they have to decide early on whether their data is going to live in a file system or an object store, and to be stuck with the consequences of that from then on. With that decision, they are basically picking how they are going to interact with their data not just now, but long into the future, and if they get it wrong they either have to do a migration or build a layer of automation for copying data.Early on, the idea was basically that we would just put EFS and S3 in a giant pot, simmer it for a bit, and we would get the best of both worlds. We even called the early version of the project “EFS3” (and I’m glad we didn’t keep that name!). But things got tricky in a hurry. Every time we sat down to work through designs, we found difficult technical challenges and tough decisions. And in each of these decisions, either the file or the object presentation of data would have to give something up in the design that would make it a bit less good. One of the engineers on the team described this as “a battle of unpalatable compromises.” We were hardly the first storage people to discover how difficult it is to converge file and object into a single storage system, but we were also acutely aware of how much not having a solution to the problem was frustrating builders.We were determined to find a path through it so we did the only sensible thing you can do when you are faced with a really difficult technical design problem: we locked a bunch of our most senior engineers in a room and said we weren’t going to let them out till they had a plan that they all liked.Passionate and contentious discussions ensued. And ensued. And ensued. And eventually we gave up. We just couldn’t get to a solution that didn’t leave someone (and in most cases really everyone) unhappy with the design.A quick aside at this point: I may be taking some dramatic liberties with the comment about locking people in a room. The Amazon meeting rooms don’t have locks on them. But to be clear on this point: I frequently find that we make the fastest and most constructive progress on really hard design problems when we get smart, passionate people with differing technical views in front of a whiteboard to really dig in over a period of days. This isn’t an earth-moving observation, but it’s often surprising how easy it can be to forget in the face of trying to talk through big hard problems in one-hour blocks over video conference. The engineers in these discussions deeply understood file and object workloads and the subtleties of how different they can be, and so these discussions were deep, sometimes heated, and absolutely fascinating. And despite all of this, we still couldn’t get to a design that we liked. It was really frustrating.This was around Christmas of 2024. Leading into the holidays, the team changed course. They went through the design docs and discussion notes that they had and started to enumerate all of the specific design compromises and the behaviour that we would need to be comfortable with if we wanted to present both file and object interfaces as a single unified system. We all looked at it and agreed that it wasn’t the best of both worlds, it was the lowest common denominator, and we could all think of example workloads on both sides that would break in surprising, often subtle, and always frustrating ways.I think the example where this really stood out to me was around the top-level semantics and experience of how objects and files are actually different as data primitives. Here’s a painfully simple characterization: files are an operating system construct. They exist on storage, and persist when the power is out, but when they are used they are incredibly rich as a way of representing data, to the point that they are very frequently used as a way of communicating across threads, processes, and applications. Application APIs for files are built to support the idea that I can update a record in a database in place, or append data to a log, and that you can concurrently access that file and see my change almost instantaneously, to an arbitrary sub-region of the file. There’s a rich set of OS functionality, like mmap() that doubles down on files as shared persistent data that can mutate at a very fine granularity and as if it is a set of in-memory data structures.Now if we flip over to object world, the idea of writing to the middle of an object while someone else is accessing it is more or less sacrilege. The immutability of objects is an assumption that is cooked into APIs and applications. Tools will download and verify content hashes, they will use object versioning to preserve old copies. Most notable of all, they often build sophisticated and complex workflows that are entirely anchored on the notifications that are associated with whole object creation. This last thing was something that surprised me when I started working on S3, and it’s actually really cool. Systems like S3 Cross Region Replication (CRR) replicate data based on notifications that happen when objects are created or overwritten and those notifications are counted on to have at-least-once semantics in order to ensure that we never miss replication for an object. Customers use similar pipelines to trigger log processing, image transcoding and all sorts of other stuff–it’s a very popular pattern for application design over objects. In fact, notifications are an example of an S3 subsystem that makes me marvel at the scale of the storage system I get to work on: S3 sends over 300 billion event notifications every day just to serverless event listeners that process new objects!The thing that we came to realize was that there is actually a pretty profound boundary between files and objects. File interactions are agile, often mutation heavy, and semantically rich. Objects on the other hand come with a relatively focused and narrow set of semantics; and we realized that this boundary that separated them was what we really needed to pay attention to, and that rather than trying to hide it, the boundary itself was the feature we needed to build.Stage and CommitWhen we got back from the holidays, we started locking (well, ok, not exactly locking) folks in rooms again, but this time with the view that the boundary between file and object didn’t actually have to be invisible. And this time, the team started coming out of discussions looking a lot happier.The first decision was that we were going to treat first-class file access on S3 as a presentation layer for working with data. We would allow customers to define an S3 mount on a bucket or prefix, and that under the covers, that mount would attach an EFS namespace to mirror the metadata from S3. We would make the transit and consistency of data across the two layers an absolutely central part of our design. We started to describe this as “stage and commit,” a term that we borrowed from version control systems like git—changes would be able to accumulate in EFS, and then be pushed down collectively to S3—and that the specifics of how and when data transited the boundary would be published as part of the system, clear to customers, and something that we could actually continue to evolve and improve as a programmatic primitive over time. (I’m going to talk about this point a little more at the end, because there’s much more the team is excited to do on this surface).Being explicit about the boundary between file and object presentations is something that I did not expect at all when the team started working on S3 Files, and it’s something that I’ve really come to love about the design. It is early and there is plenty of room for us to evolve, but I think the team all feels that it sets us up on a path where we are excited to improve and evolve in partnership with what builders need, and not be stuck behind those unpalatable compromises. Not out of the woodsDeciding on this stage and commit thing was one of those design decisions that provided some boundaries and separation of concerns. It gave us a clear structure, but it didn’t make the hard problems go away. The team still had to navigate real tradeoffs between file and object semantics, performance, and consistency. Let me walk through a few examples to show how nuanced these two abstractions really are, and how the team approached these decisions.Consistency and atomicityS3 readers often assume full object updates, notifications, and in many cases access to historical versions. File systems have fine-grained mutations, but they have important consistency and atomicity tricks as well. Many applications depend on the ability to do atomic file renames as a way of making a large change visible all at once. They do the same thing with directory moves. S3 conditionals help a bit with the first thing but aren’t an exact match, and there isn’t an S3 analog for the second. So as mentioned above, separating the layers allows these modalities to coexist in parallel systems with a single view of the same data. You can mutate and rename a file all you want, and at a later point, it will be written as a whole to S3.AuthorizationAuthorization is equally thorny. S3 and file systems think about authorization in very different ways. S3 supports IAM policies scoped to key prefixes—you can say “deny GetObject on anything under /private/”. In fact, you can further constrain those permissions based on things like the network or properties of the request itself. IAM policies are incredibly rich, and also much more expensive to evaluate than file permissions are. File systems have spent years getting things like permission checks off of the data path, often evaluating up front and then using a handle for persistent future access. Files are also a little weird as an entity to wrap authorization policy around, because permissions for a file live in its inode. Hard links allow you to have many inodes for the same file, and you also need to think about directory permissions that determine if you can get to a file in the first place. Unless you have a handle on it, in which case it kind of doesn’t matter, even if it’s renamed, moved, and often even deleted.There’s a lot more complexity, erm, richness to discuss here—especially around topics like user and group identity—but by moving to an explicit boundary, the team got themselves out of having to co-represent both types of permissions on every single object. Instead, permissions could be specified on the mount itself (familiar territory for network file system users) and enforced within the file system, with specific mappings applied across the two worlds.This design had another advantage. It preserved IAM policy on S3 as a backstop. You can always disable access at the S3 layer if you need to change a data perimeter, while delegating authorization up to the file layer within each mount. And it left the door open for situations in the future where we might want to explore multiple different mounts over the same data.The dreadful incongruity of namespace semanticsIf you are familiar with both file and object systems, it’s not a hard exercise to think about cases where file and object naming behaves quite differently. When you start to sit down and really dig into it, things get almost hilariously desolate. File systems have first-class path separators—often forward slash ("/") characters. S3 has these too, but they are really just a suggestion. In fact, S3’s LIST command allows you to specify anything you want to be parsed as a path separator and there are a handful of customers who have built remarkable multi-dimensional naming structures that embed multiple different separators in the same paths and pass a different delimiter to LIST depending on how they want to organize results.Here’s another simple and annoying one: because S3 doesn’t have directories, you can have objects that end with that same slash. That’s to say, that you can have a thing that looks like a directory but is a file. For about 20 minutes the team thought this was a cool feature and were calling them “filerectories.” Thank goodness we didn’t keep that one.There are tens of these differences, and we carefully thought about restricting to a single common structure or just fixing ourselves on one side or the other. On all of these paths we realized that we were going to break assumptions about naming inside applications.We decided to lean into the boundary and allow both sides to stick with their existing naming conventions and semantics. When objects or files are created that can’t be moved across the boundary, we decided that (and wow was this ever a lot of passionate discussion) we just wouldn’t move them. Instead, we would emit an event to allow customers to monitor and take action if necessary. This is clearly an example of downloading complexity onto the developer, but I think it’s also a profoundly good example of that being the right thing to do, because we are choosing not to fail things in the domains where they already expect to run, we are building a boundary that admits the vast majority of path names that actually do work in both cases, and we are building a mechanism to detect and correct problems as they arise.The experience of performanceThe last big area of differences that the team spent a lot of time talking about was performance, and in particular the performance and request latency of namespace interactions. File and object namespaces are optimized for very different things. In a file system, there are a lot of data-dependent accesses to metadata. Accessing a file means also accessing (and in some cases updating) the directory record. There are also many operations that end up traversing all of the directory records along a path. As a result, fast file system namespaces—even big distributed ones, tend to co-locate all the metadata for a directory on a single host so that those interactions are as fast as possible. The object namespace is completely flat and tends to optimize for very highly parallel point queries and updates. There are many cases in S3 where individual “directories” have billions of objects in them and are being accessed by hundreds of thousands of clients in parallel.As we looked through the set of challenges that I’ve just described, we spent a lot of time talking about adoption. S3 is two decades old and we wanted a solution that existing S3 customers could immediately use on their own data, and not one that meant migrating to something completely new. There are enormous numbers of existing buckets serving applications that depend on S3’s object semantics working exactly as documented. We were not willing to introduce subtle new behaviours that could break those applications.It turns out that very few applications use both file and object interfaces concurrently on the same data at the same instant. The far more common pattern is multiphase. A data processing pipeline uses filesystem tools in one stage to produce output that’s consumed by object-based applications in the next. Or a customer wants to run analytics queries over a snapshot of data that’s actively being modified through a filesystem.We realized that it’s not necessary to converge file and object semantics to solve the data silo problem. What they needed was the same data in one place, with the right view for each access pattern. A file view that provides full NFS close-to-open consistency. An object view that provides full S3 atomic-PUT strong consistency. And a synchronization layer that keeps them connected.So we shipped itAll of that arguing—the team’s list of “unpalatable compromises”, the passionate and occasionally desolate discussions about filerectories—turned out to be exactly the work we needed to do. I think the team all feels that the design is better for having gone through it. S3 Files lets you mount any S3 bucket or prefix as a filesystem on your EC2 instance, container, or Lambda function. Behind the scenes it’s backed by EFS, which provides the file experience your tools already expect. NFS semantics, directory operations, permissions. From your application’s perspective, it’s a mounted directory. From S3’s perspective, the data is objects in a bucket.The way it works is worth a quick walk through. When you first access a directory, S3 Files imports metadata from S3 and populates a synchronized view. For files under 128 KB it also pulls the data itself. For larger files only metadata comes over and the data is fetched from S3 when you actually read it. This lazy hydration is important because it means that you can mount a bucket with millions of objects in it and just start working immediately. This “start working immediately” part is a good example of a simple experience that is actually pretty sophisticated under the covers–being able to mount and immediately work with objects in S3 as files is an obvious and natural expectation for the feature, and it would be pretty frustrating to have to wait minutes or hours for the file view of metadata to be populated. But under the covers, S3 Files needs to scan S3 metadata and populate a file-optimized namespace for it, and the team was able to make this happen very quickly, and as a background operation that preserves a simple and very agile customer experience.When you create or modify files, changes are aggregated and committed back to S3 roughly every 60 seconds as a single PUT. Sync runs in both directions, so when other applications modify objects in the bucket, S3 Files automatically spots those modifications and reflects them in the filesystem view automatically. If there is ever a conflict where files are modified from both places at the same time, S3 is the source of truth and the filesystem version moves to a lost+found directory with a CloudWatch metric identifying the event. File data that hasn’t been accessed in 30 days is evicted from the filesystem view but not deleted from S3, so storage costs stay proportional to your active working set.There are many smaller, and really fun bits of work that happened as the team built the system. One of the improvements that I think is really cool is what we are calling “read bypass.” For high-throughput sequential reads, read bypass automatically reroutes the read data path to not use traditional NFS access, and instead to perform parallel GET requests directly to S3 itself, this approach achieves 3 GB/s per client (with further room to improve) and scales to terabits per second across multiple clients. And for those who are interested, there’s way more detail in our technical docs (which are a pretty interesting read).One thing I’ve really come to appreciate about the design is how honest it is about its own edges. The explicit boundary between file and object domains isn’t a limitation we’re papering over. It’s the thing that lets both sides remain uncompromised. That said, there are places where we know we still have work to do. Renames are expensive because S3 has no native rename operation, so renaming a directory means copying and deleting every object under that prefix. We warn you when a mount covers more than 50 million objects for exactly this reason. Explicit commit control isn’t there at launch; the 60-second window works for most workloads but we know it won’t be enough for everyone. And there are object keys that simply can’t be represented as valid POSIX filenames, so they won’t appear in the filesystem view. We’ve been in customer beta for about nine months and these are the things that we’ve learned and continued to evolve and iterate on with early customers. We’d rather be clear about them than pretend they don’t exist.Files and SunflowersWhen we were working with Loren’s lab at UBC, JS spent a remarkable amount of his time building caching and naming layers – not doing biology, but writing infrastructure to shuttle data between where it lived and where tools expected it to be. That friction really stood out to me, and looking back at it now, I think the lesson we kept learning – in that lab, and then over and over again as the S3 team worked on Tables, Vectors, and now Files – is that different ways of working with data aren’t a problem to be collapsed. They’re a reality to be served. The sunflowers in Loren’s lab thrived on variation, and it turns out data access patterns do too.What I find most exciting about S3 Files is something I genuinely did not expect when we started: that the explicit boundary between file and object turned out to be the best part of the design. We spent months trying to make it disappear, and when we finally accepted it as a first-class element of the system, everything got better. Stage and commit gives us a surface that we can continue to evolve – more control over when and how data transits the boundary, richer integration with pipelines and workflows–and it sets us up to do that without compromising either side.20 years ago, S3 started as an object store. Over the past couple of years, with Tables, Vectors, and now Files, it’s become something broader. A place where data lives durably and can be worked with in whatever way makes sense for the job at hand. Our goal is for the storage system to get out of the way of your work, not to be a thing that you have to work around. We’re nowhere near done, but I’m really excited about the direction that we’re heading in.As Werner says, “Now, go build!”

评论（默认中文）

MontyCarloHall 2026/04/08 05:12
这本质上是 S3FS，使用 EFS（AWS 的托管 NFS 服务）作为活动数据和小型随机访问的缓存层。不幸的是，这也意味着它附带了 EFS 的一些令人眼花缭乱的定价：

— 所有写入成本为 0.06 美元/GB，因为所有内容都会首先写入 EFS 缓存。对于写入量大的应用程序来说，这可能会造成破坏。

— 读取命中缓存的费用为 0.03 美元/GB。大量读取 (>128kB) 直接从底层 S3 存储桶中传输，这是免费的。

— 缓存收费为 0.30 美元/GB/月。尽管所有内容都写入缓存（出于一致性目的），但它似乎仅用于小文件（<128kB）的持久存储，因此这不会花费太多。

This is essentially S3FS using EFS (AWS's managed NFS service) as a cache layer for active data and small random accesses. Unfortunately, this also means that it comes with some of EFS's eye-watering pricing:

— All writes cost $0.06/GB, since everything is first written to the EFS cache. For write-heavy applications, this could be a dealbreaker.

— Reads hitting the cache get billed at $0.03/GB. Large reads (>128kB) get directly streamed from the underlying S3 bucket, which is free.

— Cache is charged at $0.30/GB/month. Even though everything is written to the cache (for consistency purposes), it seems like it's only used for persistent storage of small files (<128kB), so this shouldn't cost too much.

ktimespi 2026/04/08 10:27
这也是我关心的问题。使用 S3 作为文件系统而不是 EBS / EFS（至少对我来说）的全部目的是最大限度地降低成本，我真的不明白为什么我会使用它而不是 s3fs。
This was my concern too. The whole point of using S3 as a file system instead of EBS / EFS (for me at least) is to minimize cost and I don't really see why I would use this instead of s3fs.

the8472 2026/04/08 06:39
> 大量读取 (>128kB) 直接从底层 S3 存储桶传输，这是免费的。

总是未缓存？ S3 的延迟非常糟糕。

> Large reads (>128kB) get directly streamed from the underlying S3 bucket, which is free.

Always uncached? S3 has pretty bad latency.

在 HN 中查看
MontyCarloHall 2026/04/08 07:27
使用缓存的阈值是可配置的，默认为 128kB。假设任何大于阈值的读取都将是长时间持续读取，对此延迟并不太重要。我的问题是，从 >128kB 的文件中读取 <128kB（或任何阈值）是否会保存到缓存中，或者仅用于总体大小低于阈值的文件？对大文件的频繁随机访问是此类缓存层的教科书用例，但在该系统中其成本将是巨大的。
The threshold at which the cache gets used is configurable, with 128kB the default. The assumption is that any read larger than the threshold will be a long sustained read, for which latency doesn't matter too much. My question is, do reads <128kB (or whatever the threshold is) from files >128kB get saved to the cache, or is it only used for files whose overall size is under the threshold? Frequent random access to large files is a textbook use case for a caching layer like this, but its cost will be substantial in this system.
在 HN 中查看
the8472 2026/04/08 07:55
对于 128kB 块，NVMe 读取延迟在 10-100μs 范围内。 S3约为100ms。那是 3-4 个 OOM。 总读取持续时间开始主导延迟的阈值将在数十到数百兆字节，而不是千字节。
NVMe read latency is in the 10-100µs range for 128kB blocks. S3 is about 100ms. That's 3-4 OOMs. The threshold where the total read duration starts to dominate latency would be somewhere in the dozens to hundreds of megabytes, not kilobytes.

MontyCarloHall 2026/04/08 08:08
我同意，这是一个奇怪的低门槛。 NFS 与 S3 的延迟差异是几个 OOM，因此 ~10MB 的阈值对我来说似乎更合适。也许故意将其设置得较低以避免产生巨额 EFS 账单？将其设置得更高实际上意味着对大部分读取收取 0.03 美元/GB 的费用，这对于大多数人的应用程序来说是站不住脚的。
I agree, it's an oddly low threshold. The latency differential of NFS vs. S3 is a couple OOMs, so a threshold of ~10MB seems more appropriate to me. Perhaps it's set intentionally low to avoid racking up immense EFS bills? Setting it higher would effectively mean getting billed $0.03/GB for a huge fraction of reads, which is untenable for most people's applications.

Aren't you comparing local in-process latency to network latency? That's multiple OOM right there.

the8472 2026/04/08 08:41
不会，在相同的 DC 网络中，延迟不会增加那么多。毕竟 EFS 还可以管理 600μs 的平均延迟。 实际上只是 S3 慢。我假设 S3 的很大一部分分布在 HDD 上，而不是 SSD 上。
No, within the same DC network latency does not add that much. After all EFS also manages 600µs average latency. It's really just S3 that's slow. I assume some large fraction of S3 is spread over HDDs, not SSDs.

everfrustrated 2026/04/08 10:40
考虑此架构的最佳方式是它具有与 S3 双向同步的 EFS。

您可以向其中一个写入并从另一个读出，反之亦然。一致性保证在每个内部而不是之间进行。

The best way to think of the architecture of this is it's EFS with a bidirectional sync to S3.

You can write into one and read out from the other and vice versa. Consistency guarantees kept within each but not between.

rdtsc 2026/04/08 05:12
同步位是我想知道的： https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/s3-fil...

> 例如，假设您通过文件系统编辑 /mnt/s3files/report.csv。在 S3 Files 将您的更改同步回 S3 存储桶之前，另一个应用程序会将新版本的 report.csv 直接上传到 S3 存储桶。当 S3 Files 检测到冲突时，它会将您的 report.csv 版本移至 Lost and Find 目录，并将其替换为 S3 存储桶中的版本。

> 丢失和找到的目录位于文件系统的根目录中，名称为 .s3files-lost+found-file-system-id。

Synchronization bits is what I was wondering about: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/s3-fil...

> For example, suppose you edit /mnt/s3files/report.csv through the file system. Before S3 Files synchronizes your changes back to the S3 bucket, another application uploads a new version of report.csv directly to the S3 bucket. When S3 Files detects the conflict, it moves your version of report.csv to the lost and found directory and replaces it with the version from the S3 bucket.

> The lost and found directory is located in your file system's root directory under the name .s3files-lost+found-file-system-id.

dabinat 2026/04/08 09:31
使用 S3 作为文件系统的问题在于它是不可变的，并且 S3 文件也没有改变这一点。因此，如果我有一个大文件并更改了其中的 1 个字节，甚至只是重命名，则需要重新上传整个文件。这对于读取大量文件（足够小以适合缓存的文件）的工作流程似乎最有用。
The problem with using S3 as a filesystem is that it’s immutable, and that hasn’t changed with S3 Files. So if I have a large file and change 1 byte of it, or even just rename it, it needs to upload the entire file all over again. This seems most useful for read-heavy workflows of files that are small enough to fit in the cache.

wolttam 2026/04/08 09:40
这与 CoW 文件系统没有什么不同——没有文件必须 1:1 映射到对象的规则；您可以（透明地）将文件分成更小的块，以进行更细粒度的编辑。
That’s not that different than CoW filesystems - there is no rule that files must map 1:1 to objects; you can (transparently) divide a file into smaller chunks to enable more fine grained edits.

jitl 2026/04/08 05:31
我希望他们提供一些到本地 NVMe 存储的托管桥接。与 EBS 相比，AWS NVMe 速度超快，并且 EBS（作为块设备的节点独占访问）比 EFS（多节点访问）更快。我想如果你在上面放置某种进一步缓存到 NVMe FS 的东西，这个过程会很快，但完全垂直集成的选项会更好。
I wish they offered some managed bridging to local NVMe storage. AWS NVMe is super fast compared to EBS, and EBS (node-exclusive access as block device) is faster than EFS (multi-node access). I imagine this can go fast if you put some kind of further-cache-to-NVMe FS on top, but a completely vertically integrated option would be much better.
在 HN 中查看
MontyCarloHall 2026/04/08 07:45
由于 EFS 只是一个 NFS 挂载，我想知道您是否可以通过将 NVMe 卷附加到您的实例并在 NFS 挂载上设置诸如 cachefilesd 之类的东西来自己完成此操作，指向 NVMe。

会

   mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme0n1 && \
   mount /dev/nvme0n1 /var/cache/fscache && \
   mount -t s3files -o fsc fs-0aa860d05df9afdfe:/ /home/ec2-user/s3files
开箱即用？对于 EFS 来说确实如此。提供一个实际上由三个 shell 命令组成的托管服务似乎不值得，但是这个 是 我们正在谈论的AWS。
Since EFS is just an NFS mount, I wonder if you could do this yourself by attaching an NVMe volume to your instance and setting up something like cachefilesd on the NFS mount, pointed to the NVMe.

Would

   mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme0n1 && \
   mount /dev/nvme0n1 /var/cache/fscache && \
   mount -t s3files -o fsc fs-0aa860d05df9afdfe:/ /home/ec2-user/s3files
work out of the box? It does for EFS. It hardly seems worth it to offer a managed service that's effectively three shell commands, but this is AWS we're talking about.
在 HN 中查看
jitl 2026/04/08 09:45
AWS 的 [EFS 性能文档](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/performance-tips.h...） 说：

> 不要使用以下安装选项：

> - fsc – 此选项启用本地文件缓存，但不会更改 NFS 缓存一致性，也不会减少延迟。

如果 S3 文件同步逻辑在客户端运行，我们几乎可以完全避免缓存文件的文件访问延迟并支付新的昂贵 EFS 磁盘的费用。我已经支付了很多 NVMe 磁盘的费用，让我使用这些吧！

AWS's [docs on EFS performance](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/performance-tips.h...) say:

> Don't use the following mount options:

> - fsc – This option enables local file caching, but does not change NFS cache coherency, and does not reduce latencies.

If the S3 Files sync logic ran client-side, we could almost entirely avoid file access latency for cached files and paying for new expensive EFS disks. I already pay for a lot of NVMe disks, let me just use those!

在 HN 中查看
MontyCarloHall 2026/04/08 10:01
>此选项启用本地文件缓存，但不会更改 NFS 缓存一致性，也不会减少延迟。

对于任何 NFS 设置都是如此，而不仅仅是 EFS。本地 NFS 缓存的好处是加快大型、不可变文件的读取速度，其中延迟相对可以忽略不计。我不确定为什么 AWS 专门劝阻用户启用缓存，因为 EFS 卷的带宽甚至不在 EBS/NVMe 带宽的范围内。

>This option enables local file caching, but does not change NFS cache coherency, and does not reduce latencies.

That's true for any NFS setup, not just EFS. The benefit of local NFS caching is to speed up reads of large, immutable files, where latency is relatively negligible. I'm not sure why AWS specifically dissuades users from enabling caching, since it's not like bandwidth to an EFS volume is even in the ballpark of EBS/NVMe bandwidth.

在 HN 中查看
yandie 2026/04/08 04:35
看来他们在 S3 前面放置了一个实际的文件系统（基本上是 AWS EFS），然后执行透明同步。该博客文章讨论了许多注意事项（例如一致性）或对象命名（不一致作为事件发送给客户）。

作为 S3 的粉丝这么久了，我真的很喜欢它的设计。这是一个很好的妥协，对于成功完成设计的人来说是值得赞扬的。

It appears that they put an actual file system in front of S3 (AWS EFS basically) and then perform transparent syncing. The blog post discusses a lot of caveats (consistency, for example) or object namings (incosistencies are emitted as events to customers).

Having been a fan of S3 for such a long time, I'm really a fan of the design. It's a good compromise and kudos to whoever managed to push through the design.

在 HN 中查看
PedroBatista 2026/04/08 07:21
人，我指的是大客户组织 ( $$$ ) 的架构师和首席开发人员一直在使用 S3 作为文件系统，作为他们通常古怪的大型复杂项目的支柱之一。

因此，AWS 一直面临着让它像这样工作的压力。我怀疑 AWS 收到的支持票数量与“我的 S3 支持的项目很慢/有时会失败/遇到 AWS 限制（例如每个帐户的最大存储桶数量）”和“为什么不......”设计阶段的问题（很多时候 AWS 人员都在房间里）有关，足以长期施加压力来克服 S3 的技术限制。

我不喜欢这种“让我们在上面穿上一件新外套，假装它根本不是”的抽象概念。但我怀疑这是一个由$$$ 收取社会压力涡轮的案例。

People and by people I mean architects and lead devs at big account orgs ( $$$ ) have been using S3 as a filesystem as one of the backbones of their usually wacky mega complex projects.

So there always been a pressure to AWS make it work like that. I suspect the amount of support tickets AWS receives related to "My S3 backed project is slow/fails sometimes/run into AWS limits (like the max number of buckets per account)" and "Why don't.." questions in the design phase which many times AWS people are in the room, serve as enough of a long applied pressure to overcome technical limitations of S3.

I'm not a fan of this type of "let's put a fresh coat on top of it and pretend it's something that fundamentally is not" abstractions. But I suspect here is a case of social pressure turbo charged by $$$.

munk-a 2026/04/08 07:51
我认为这为他们打开了一个庞大的客户群，其中包括技术不太熟练的人，他们只是随机下载了一些“S3asYourFS.exe”程序，但也让他们需要支持该功能以及来自技术不太熟练的人的现场支持电话。 我不知道这个商业决策是否有意义（因为 AWS 已经缺乏 CS 基础设施，甚至无法与专业客户打交道），但是让每个人和他们的兄弟向 AWS 支付月费的想法可能太诱人了，不容错过。
I think it opens them up to a huge customer base of less technically apt people who just downloaded some random "S3asYourFS.exe" program but also opens them up to needing to support that functionality and field support calls from less technically apt people. I don't know if that business decision makes sense (since AWS already lacks the CS infrastructure to even deal with professional clients) but the idea that you could get everyone and their brother paying monthly fees to AWS is likely too tempting of a fruit to pass up.

jitl 2026/04/08 05:24
因为如果没有大量的工程工作（请参阅博客文章），对象存储语义和文件语义之间的不匹配意味着您可能会度过一段糟糕的时光。在 S3 的早期时代，还存在一些实现细节，例如基于关键前缀的吞吐量限制（该前缀大约在 2016 年消失），这使得用于分层目录形状的情况变得更糟。
Because without significant engineering effort (see the blog post), the mismatch between object store semantics and file semantics mean you will probably Have A Bad Time. In much earlier eras of S3, there were also some implementation specifics like throughput limits based on key prefixes (that one vanished circa 2016) that made it even worse to use for hierarchical directory shapes.

在 HN 中查看
huntaub 2026/04/08 04:52
这与 s3fs 有很大不同。 s3fs 是由 S3 支持的 FUSE 文件系统。

这意味着您可能想要在 S3 上执行的所有非原子操作（包括对文件中间的编辑、重命名等）都在运行 S3fs 的计算机上运行。因此，如果您的计算机崩溃，则不清楚 S3 存储桶中会显示什么内容，也不清楚是否会损坏内容。

因此，S3fs 也是 慢的 因为这意味着您的计算机之后的下一站是 S3，这不适合许多基于文件的应用程序。

AWS 在这里构建的内容有所不同，使用 EFS 作为中间层意味着您的文件系统操作在对象操作中组装时有一个安全、持久的位置。这也意味着性能应该比 s3fs 好得多（它与数据相距 1 毫秒的 SSD 通信，而不是与数据相距 30 毫秒的 HDD 通信）。

This is pretty different than s3fs. s3fs is a FUSE file system that is backed by S3.

This means that all of the non-atomic operations that you might want to do on S3 (including edits to the middle of files, renames, etc) are run on the machine running S3fs. As a result, if your machine crashes, it's not clear what's going to show up in your S3 bucket or if would corrupt things.

As a result, S3fs is also slow because it means that the next stop after your machine is S3, which isn't suitable for many file-based applications.

What AWS has built here is different, using EFS as the middle layer means that there's a safe, durable place for your file system operations to go while they're being assembled in object operations. It also means that the performance should be much better than s3fs (it's talking to ssds where data is 1ms away instead of hdds where data is 30ms away).

在 HN 中查看
themafia 2026/04/08 04:01
> 我们把一群最高级的工程师锁在一个房间里，并说我们不会让他们出去，除非他们有一个他们都喜欢的计划。

这是一种方法。

> 当您创建或修改文件时，大约每 60 秒就会聚合更改并作为单个 PUT 提交回 S3。同步是双向运行的，因此当其他应用程序修改存储桶中的对象时，S3 Files 会自动发现这些修改并自动将它们反映在文件系统视图中。

考虑到上述情况，这听起来是正确的。 我很难将其视为一个巨大的“黑客攻击”。 我已经不喜欢预测新型 S3 访问模式的成本，并且我觉得有可能使我已经经历过的复杂性增加一倍。

也许我太节俭了，但我已经在云端工作了十年，而且我非常努力地防止出现任何“意外”账单。 这看起来是一个很棒的功能；如果您不在乎每月的 AWS 账单是多少。

> we locked a bunch of our most senior engineers in a room and said we weren’t going to let them out till they had a plan that they all liked.

That's one way to do it.

> When you create or modify files, changes are aggregated and committed back to S3 roughly every 60 seconds as a single PUT. Sync runs in both directions, so when other applications modify objects in the bucket, S3 Files automatically spots those modifications and reflects them in the filesystem view automatically.

That sounds about right given the above. I have trouble seeing this as something other than a giant "hack." I already don't enjoy projecting costs for new types of S3 access patterns and I feel like has the potential to double the complication I already experience here.

Maybe I'm too frugal, but I've been in the cloud for a decade now, and I've worked very hard to prevent any "surprise" bills from showing up. This seems like a great feature; if you don't care what your AWS bill is each month.

hiyer 2026/04/08 07:21
我也考虑将它与 Duckdb 一起使用，但似乎效果有限。 Parquet 对象以 MB 为单位，因此它们将直接从 S3 流式传输。对于原始镶木地板对象，如果您有很多此类对象，可能会对 S3 列表有所帮助（可以缩短查询时间）。如果您已经在 Ducklake 上，Duckdb 无论如何都会使用它来获取相关对象的列表。
I was thinking of using it with Duckdb as well but seems it would be of limited benefit. Parquet objects are in MBs, so they would be streamed directly from S3. With raw parquet objects, it might help with S3 listing if you have a lot of them (shave off a couple of seconds from the query). If you are already on Ducklake, Duckdb will use that for getting the list of relevant objects anyway.

wenc 2026/04/08 09:01
也许OP正在考虑读/写DuckDB本机格式文件。这些需要文件系统语义来进行写入。不幸的是，对于 DuckDB 来说，即使是 NFS 或 SMB 也不够像 FS。

Parquet 仅是静态追加，因此 DuckDB 对于那些生活在 S3 上的人来说没有问题。

Maybe the OP is thinking of reading/writing to DuckDB native format files. Those require filesystem semantics for writing. Unfortunately, even NFS or SMB are not sufficiently FS-like for DuckDB.

Parquet is static append only, so DuckDB has no problems with those living on S3.

CrzyLngPwd 2026/04/08 04:13
如果有一篇文章需要 TLDR 或 AI 摘要，那就是它。

出售利益。

我在 S3 上有大约 9 TB 的 2100 万个文件。这个改变对我有什么好处？

If there is ever a post that needs a TLDR or an AI summary it is that one.

Sell the benefits.

I have around 9 TB in 21m files on S3. How does this change benefit me?

dijksterhuis 2026/04/08 04:55
并不是所有的东西都应该或需要是一些旨在方便观众的文章，或者向观众推销一些东西。几乎所有 allthingsdistributed 文章都是长篇文章，涵盖高度技术性的系统，并包含大量的细节/上下文。在我看来，与“React 提高页面访问量的 5 种方法”列表相比，它们更接近于“计算机科学家发表博客文章”。

稍作编辑...我真的需要重新打开 10 分钟的延迟。

not everything should or needs to be some article geared towards the audience's convenience, or selling something to the audience. pretty much all allthingsdistributed articles are long form articles covering highly technical systems and contain a decent whack of detail/context. in my mind, they veer closer to "computer scientist does blog posts" compared to "5 ways React can boost your page visits" listicles.

edited slightly ... i really need to turn 10 minute post delay back on.
